title: The Interwar Period
summary: The twenty years between the two World Wars — a period of democratic experimentation, fascist and communist revolution, economic catastrophe, and the failure of the international order created at Versailles — that ended with the most destructive war in human history.
hook: The interwar period began with the most ambitious attempt to create a peaceful international order in modern history and ended with the most catastrophic failure of that attempt. Twenty years separated the Treaty of Versailles from the German invasion of Poland. Within that time, democracy collapsed across most of Europe, the global economy imploded, and the conditions for genocide and world war were assembled piece by piece.

beginner.thePeriod:
The interwar period sits between two catastrophes — the First World War and the Second. This has given it a peculiar quality in historical memory. Looking back from 1945, the twenty years between 1919 and 1939 seem to march inevitably toward their conclusion. The tragedies appear foreordained.

They were not. The 1920s had genuine stability and real hope. The 1930s had alternatives to fascism at every turn. The disaster was not determined in advance.

The interwar period — roughly 1919 to 1939 — was a time of extraordinary volatility: democratic hope and fascist reaction, economic boom and catastrophic bust, artistic achievement and political violence. Understanding it requires resisting the pull of hindsight.
beginner.theBoundaries:
The period begins with the Paris Peace Conference of 1919 and the treaties that ended the First World War — including Versailles, which reshaped the map of Europe. It ends with Germany's invasion of Poland on September 1, 1939.

Within these twenty years, historians identify two distinct phases. The 1920s saw genuine stabilization: economic recovery, democratic consolidation in several countries, and a spirit of diplomatic reconciliation centered on the 1925 Locarno agreements. The 1930s saw this stability destroyed by the Great Depression, which created the conditions for fascism's triumph in Germany and the collapse of collective security.

The conventional picture — prosperous 1920s followed by desperate 1930s — is geographically limited. Germany, Austria, and much of Eastern Europe experienced severe economic instability throughout the 1920s. The turning point was not 1929 but 1931, when a German banking crisis converted an American stock market crash into a European political catastrophe.
beginner.theConditions:
The interwar period was shaped by four interlocking crises.

The political crisis was the failure of democratic institutions across most of Europe. By 1939, only France, Britain, the Scandinavian countries, the Low Countries, and Switzerland remained functioning democracies on the continent.

The economic crisis was the Great Depression. After 1929, mass unemployment became the defining political fact of the 1930s. Governments that could not solve it lost legitimacy. Movements that promised to solve it — including fascist ones — gained support.

The security crisis was the failure of collective security. The League of Nations could not stop Japanese expansion in Manchuria in 1931, Italian aggression in Ethiopia in 1935, or German rearmament and expansion from 1936 onward.

The ideological crisis was the competition between liberal democracy, fascism, and communism, each claiming to represent the future. The 1930s felt like a race — and for much of the decade, liberal democracy appeared to be losing it.
beginner.internalDiversity:
The interwar period was not uniformly grim. The 1920s produced some of the 20th century's most remarkable cultural achievements — jazz, modernist literature, avant-garde film, the Harlem Renaissance, Weimar culture in Germany. Women's suffrage spread across Europe and North America. Labor unions won significant rights. Social democratic parties built the foundations of the welfare state in several countries.

The period's experience also varied enormously by geography. The United States remained a democracy and, after the New Deal, developed a distinctive American response to the Depression. Britain and France maintained democratic governance despite crises. The Scandinavian countries developed social democratic welfare states that would outlast the period.

The diversity of the interwar experience complicates the narrative of inexorable march toward catastrophe. The catastrophe was real. But it was not the only story.
beginner.longConsequences:
The interwar period's consequences were written into the world that followed it.

The failure of collective security shaped the United Nations system. The Great Depression shaped Keynesian economics and the postwar commitment to full employment. The rise of fascism shaped the decision to embed West Germany in European and Atlantic institutions rather than leaving it isolated as a revisionist power.

The period also generated a set of political lessons that have been invoked in every subsequent international crisis: about appeasement, economic policy, collective security, and democratic resilience. The most powerful of these — that appeasing an aggressive dictator produces not peace but emboldened aggression — has been used to justify military interventions for eighty years. Sometimes it was the right lesson. Sometimes it was the wrong lesson applied to a different situation.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
The central debate is whether the catastrophe was inevitable.

Eric Hobsbawm treated the interwar period as an "age of catastrophe" in which the contradictions of the post-1919 settlement inevitably generated fascism and war. The system, on this account, was doomed from the start.

Zara Steiner argues the opposite. The 1920s contained genuine possibilities for stability. The catastrophe resulted from specific failures of policy and political will that were not predetermined. Germany could have stayed democratic. Appeasement was a choice, not a necessity.

The contingency argument is more compelling. The Great Depression was not inevitable. The League of Nations could have been organized differently. Understanding the interwar period requires recovering these contingencies — treating the catastrophe as something that happened rather than something that had to happen.

general.thePeriod:
Zara Steiner's two-volume history of the interwar period is the essential scholarly account — the most comprehensive and nuanced treatment of European international history from 1919 to 1939 available in English. The Lights That Failed (2005) covers 1919–1933 and argues that the interwar order contained genuine possibilities for stability that were foreclosed by the Depression and its political consequences. The Triumph of the Dark (2011) covers 1933–1939 and analyzes how Hitler's Germany dismantled the collective security system step by step.

Eric Hobsbawm's The Age of Extremes (1994) provides the most influential single-volume account of the 20th century, with the interwar period as the central 'age of catastrophe.' Hobsbawm's Marxist framework reads the period as the crisis of capitalism that produced fascism as a response. It captures important structural dynamics while underemphasizing the contingency that Steiner's more detailed account recovers.
general.theBoundaries:
The relationship between the 1920s and the 1930s is the crucial periodization question. The conventional picture presents the 1920s as the 'Roaring Twenties' — a period of prosperity and cultural vitality — followed by the 1930s as the Depression decade. This picture is geographically limited: the 1920s prosperity was primarily American and Western European, while Germany, Austria, and much of Eastern Europe experienced severe economic instability throughout the decade. The hyperinflation that destroyed German middle-class savings in 1923 and the near-constant political crisis of Weimar Germany complicate the decade's prosperous image. So does the rural depression that affected most of Eastern Europe throughout the 1920s.

The year 1929 is conventionally treated as the decade's turning point, but the Wall Street Crash's transmission to Europe took time. The German banking crisis of 1931, which converted the American stock market crash into a European political catastrophe, is the moment when the Depression became a political crisis rather than an economic one.
general.theConditions:
Adam Tooze's The Wages of Destruction (2006) provides the essential economic history of the interwar period through the lens of the German economy. It shows how the intersection of Versailles reparations, American loans, the Depression, and Nazi rearmament shaped the choices available to German leaders. Tooze argues that the Nazi economy was structurally unsustainable — that Hitler's rearmament program was a gamble on conquest rather than a viable economic strategy. This is essential for understanding both the interwar period and the Second World War.

Barry Eichengreen's Golden Fetters (1992) provides the economic analysis of why the Great Depression was so severe. Adherence to the gold standard transmitted deflationary shocks across countries and prevented the expansionary monetary policy that would have shortened it. Countries that left gold early (Britain in 1931, the United States effectively in 1933–1934) recovered faster than those that stayed on longer. This analysis of the Depression's international transmission mechanism has become the standard account.
general.internalDiversity:
The interwar period's cultural history — modernism, surrealism, jazz, cinema — represents one of the great creative moments in Western culture. Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, T.S. Eliot, Franz Kafka, Marcel Proust, and Pablo Picasso all did their defining work in the interwar period. The Harlem Renaissance produced a new African American cultural consciousness. The cinema became mass entertainment. Radio transformed political communication — and gave demagogues like Hitler, Mussolini, and FDR new tools for reaching mass audiences.

The period's political diversity is equally important. The Spanish Civil War (1936–1939) was the clearest expression of the ideological conflict that divided the interwar period — fascism versus the Popular Front of communists, socialists, and liberals. George Orwell's Homage to Catalonia and the broader literature of the Spanish war captures the period's ideological intensity and the tragedy of the antifascist defeat.
general.longConsequences:
The lessons drawn from the interwar period have shaped international politics for eighty years. The 'Munich lesson' — that appeasing an aggressive dictator produces not peace but emboldened aggression — has been invoked to justify virtually every Western military intervention since 1945. From Korea through Vietnam to Iraq, the comparison has served to legitimate some interventions and illegitimately foreclose alternatives in others. The comparison to Munich has served both to legitimate some interventions and to illegitimately foreclose alternatives in others.

The economic lessons have been more straightforwardly absorbed. The postwar Bretton Woods system — designed to avoid the deflationary dynamics of the gold standard — reflected Keynes's interwar analysis. The commitment to full employment that characterized postwar governments in Britain, France, and elsewhere was a direct response to the political consequences of interwar mass unemployment. The European integration project was partly designed to make another European war impossible by making European economies interdependent.
general.periodizationDebate: The appeasement historiography has been one of the most contested fields in 20th-century history. The 'guilty men' tradition — established by the anonymous pamphlet Guilty Men (1940) — treated Chamberlain's appeasement as culpable and preventable. The revisionist tradition of the 1960s–1980s argued that appeasement was a rational response to genuine constraints: British military weakness, Commonwealth reluctance to fight, public opinion, and the genuine ambiguity of Hitler's intentions before the full extent of his program was clear. The post-revisionist synthesis has largely accepted the revisionist account of constraints while restoring moral judgment. Appeasement was understandable given the constraints. But those constraints were themselves partly the product of choices made in the 1920s and early 1930s that could have been made differently.

advanced.thePeriod: The historiography of the interwar period has been transformed by the opening of national archives across Europe and by the rise of transnational and global history approaches that situate European developments within broader international contexts. The 'new diplomatic history' — associated with scholars including Steiner, MacMillan, and Patrick Cohrs — recovers the genuine possibilities for stable international order in the 1920s while analyzing why they were not realized. Cohrs's The Unfinished Peace After World War I (2006) argues that the 1920s saw genuine attempts to build a durable peace that failed not because of Versailles's injustice but because of the withdrawal of American engagement and the specific contingencies of German and French domestic politics.
advanced.theBoundaries: The relationship between the interwar crisis of democracy and the broader phenomenon of democratic backsliding has become a major research area as contemporary anxieties about democracy's resilience have grown. Scholars including Sheri Berman and Anna Grzymala-Busse have analyzed the interwar period comparatively — asking why democracy survived in some countries (Britain, France, Scandinavia) and collapsed in others (Germany, Italy, Spain, Austria, Poland, Hungary). The answers emphasize the interaction of economic crisis, institutional design, political culture, and the specific choices made by political actors at key moments.
advanced.theConditions: The historiography of the Great Depression has been substantially advanced by economic historians including Barry Eichengreen, Christina Romer, and Ben Bernanke. The Depression is now understood as primarily a monetary phenomenon — the deflationary dynamics of the gold standard — rather than a structural crisis of capitalism as the Marxist tradition argued. This analysis has significant political implications: it suggests that the Depression was not inevitable and that specific policy choices (maintaining gold convertibility) made it worse than it needed to be. The political lesson — that central banks should act aggressively in financial crises rather than defending monetary orthodoxy — was explicitly invoked during the 2008 financial crisis, when Bernanke (then Fed chairman) acted on his academic analysis of the Depression.
advanced.internalDiversity: The global history of the interwar period has received increasing attention. Erez Manela's The Wilsonian Moment shows how the peace settlement disappointed colonial peoples across Asia and Africa, channeling anticolonial nationalism into specific organizational forms. Cemil Aydin's The Politics of Anti-Westernism in Asia traces how pan-Islamic and pan-Asian thought developed as responses to Western imperialism during the interwar period. The interwar period's global dimensions — the Japanese empire, the Chinese revolution, the decolonization movements — are essential for understanding the period as a world crisis rather than simply a European one.
advanced.longConsequences: The Munich comparison has been one of the most consequential and misused analogies in modern political rhetoric. The comparison — drawing lessons from Chamberlain's appeasement of Hitler for contemporary negotiations with adversaries — assumes that the structure of the current situation matches the interwar situation in relevant respects. This assumption is often unexamined and frequently wrong: not every adversary is Hitler, not every negotiation is appeasement, and the costs of military confrontation are not always lower than the costs of negotiation. The misuse of the Munich analogy — most obviously in the case of the 2003 Iraq invasion — illustrates how historical analogies can foreclose thinking rather than illuminate it.
advanced.periodizationDebate: The structured confrontation in interwar historiography is between structural accounts (the period's catastrophe was produced by the contradictions of the post-WWI settlement, the logic of capitalism in crisis, or the inherent instability of democratic governance under economic stress) and contingency accounts (the catastrophe resulted from specific policy failures and political choices that could have been made differently). Both positions have generated important scholarship. The structural accounts are essential for understanding the conditions within which political actors operated; the contingency accounts are essential for recovering the choices available within those conditions and the moral responsibility of those who made them.

foundation.theStory: The interwar period matters as the context for the 20th century's two greatest catastrophes — the Depression and the Second World War — and as the testing ground for the political, economic, and institutional lessons that shaped the postwar order. It also matters as a period whose apparent inevitability is partly an illusion: recovering the genuine contingency of the interwar period is essential for understanding that political catastrophes are not predetermined and that choices matter.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the inevitability of the period's catastrophe (structural determination versus contingency), the causes of democratic collapse across most of Europe, the economics of the Depression and its political consequences, and the lessons of appeasement for contemporary international politics.
foundation.whyItMatters: The interwar period matters for understanding the Second World War, the postwar international order, Keynesian economics, the history of fascism and communism, and the relationship between economic crisis and political extremism. Its lessons — about appeasement, economic policy, collective security, and democratic resilience — have been invoked in every subsequent international crisis.
questions: Eric Hobsbawm treated the interwar period as an 'age of catastrophe' in which the contradictions of the post-1919 settlement made fascism and war inevitable. Zara Steiner argues that the 1920s contained genuine possibilities for stability and that the catastrophe resulted from specific policy failures. Which account better fits the historical evidence?

The Munich Agreement of 1938 has become the defining symbol of appeasement — the negotiation that failed to stop Hitler and emboldened him instead. But was appeasement a rational response to genuine constraints, or was it a moral and strategic failure that could have been avoided? And when, if ever, is the Munich analogy appropriately invoked for contemporary situations?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential texts are Steiner's two volumes for the diplomatic history, Hobsbawm's Age of Extremes for the structural account, Eichengreen's Golden Fetters for the economic history, and Tooze's Wages of Destruction for the German economic story. For democratic collapse, Sheri Berman's The Primacy of Politics and Juan Linz and Alfred Stepan's comparative work on democratic breakdown are essential. For the cultural history, Modris Eksteins's Rites of Spring provides the most evocative account of the period's intellectual and cultural climate.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidential base is rich across multiple national archives. The primary limits are the incompleteness of archives from the Soviet Union and Eastern European states for parts of the period, the complexity of disaggregating the effects of different causal factors, and the difficulty of counterfactual reasoning about what different policy choices would have produced.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about the interwar period must engage with the structural versus contingency debate and take a position on the degree to which the catastrophe was predetermined. It must address the appeasement question with historical specificity — distinguishing between the constraints that made appeasement understandable and the choices that made it culpable. And it must engage with the economic history — the relationship between the Depression and political extremism — with enough precision to evaluate competing causal claims.
questions: Barry Eichengreen argues that adherence to the gold standard was the primary cause of the Depression's severity and international spread. If this analysis is correct, what does it imply about the responsibility of central bankers and governments for the political consequences of the Depression — including the rise of Hitler?

The interwar period saw democracy collapse across most of Europe while surviving in Britain, France, and Scandinavia. What explains this variation, and what does it suggest about the conditions under which democracies are resilient?

research.topic: The economics of the Great Depression and its international transmission
research.content: The economic history of the Great Depression is well established through the work of Barry Eichengreen (Golden Fetters, 1992), Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz (A Monetary History of the United States, 1963), and Christina Romer. The Depression is understood as primarily a monetary phenomenon — the gold standard's deflationary dynamics transmitted contractionary shocks across countries and prevented expansionary monetary responses. Countries that left gold early recovered faster. The US Federal Reserve's failure to prevent bank failures and its contraction of the money supply is established as a major cause of the American Depression's severity. Ben Bernanke's academic work on the Depression's transmission through banking failures informed his response as Fed chairman in 2008. The relationship between the Depression and political extremism — the timing of Nazi vote share increase matching German unemployment increase — is documented, though the causal mechanisms are more complex than simple economic determinism.

research.topic: The global history of the interwar period beyond Europe
research.content: The global dimensions of the interwar period have received increasing scholarly attention as the field has moved beyond its traditional European focus. Erez Manela's The Wilsonian Moment established how the 1919 peace settlement's failure to extend self-determination beyond Europe shaped anticolonial nationalism across Asia and Africa. Work on the Japanese empire, Chinese revolution, and Latin American political economy during the Depression has complicated the standard European-centered narrative. The interwar period looks different from the perspective of India, China, or the Middle East than from the perspective of France or Germany — it was a period of anticolonial mobilization, not simply of European democratic failure. This global history has not yet been fully integrated with the dominant European-centered accounts.

research.topic: Whether stable international order was achievable in the 1930s without the Second World War
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: detailed reconstruction of specific decision points — German rearmament (1933–1936), the Rhineland remilitarization (1936), the Anschluss (1938), Munich (1938) — combined with a causal model of how alternative decisions would have interacted with German, Italian, French, British, and Soviet domestic political constraints. Why unavailable: counterfactual historical questions are inherently underdetermined by evidence; the key constraint is that we cannot observe the alternative histories. What can be established: the specific constraints facing policymakers at each decision point; the specific choices made; and the immediate consequences. What cannot be established: whether Hitler's ambitions were satisfiable short of European hegemony — whether any settlement could have produced a stable German acceptance of the post-WWI order — which is the question that determines whether a different policy could have prevented the war.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Failure of Collective Security
comparativeNarrative.content: The liberal internationalist tradition reads the interwar period as a tragic demonstration that international order requires enforceable institutions, not just voluntary cooperation. The League of Nations failed because it lacked the power to enforce its decisions and because its most powerful potential member, the United States, never joined. The lesson drawn was the United Nations' more enforceable framework and the American commitment to international engagement that characterized the postwar order. This reading underestimates the genuine achievements of interwar diplomacy — Locarno, the Kellogg-Briand Pact, the League's successful resolution of several minor disputes — while accurately identifying the systemic weakness that allowed aggressors to operate without effective opposition.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Depression as Political Cause
comparativeNarrative.content: The economic history tradition reads the interwar catastrophe primarily through the lens of the Great Depression — arguing that the political extremism of the 1930s was substantially a consequence of mass unemployment and economic desperation rather than of ideological dynamics independent of economic conditions. The Nazi vote did not rise significantly until after 1929; it rose sharply as unemployment rose. This correlation suggests that without the Depression, Hitler might never have come to power. The implication is that the catastrophe could have been prevented by better economic policy — by an earlier departure from gold, by more expansionary fiscal policy, by better international economic coordination. This reading has significant contemporary relevance: it suggests that economic crisis is the primary threat to democratic stability.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Crisis of Liberal Capitalism
comparativeNarrative.content: The Marxist and structuralist tradition reads the interwar period as the crisis of liberal capitalism — the moment when the contradictions of the market economy, exposed by the Depression, generated fascism as capitalism's defensive response to the threat of socialist revolution. Polanyi's The Great Transformation (1944) is the foundational text: the attempt to create a self-regulating market in the 19th century generated social dislocations that provoked political reactions, and fascism was one such reaction. This reading captures genuine connections between economic transformation and political response that purely political accounts miss. Its limitation is that it tends toward structural determination, underemphasizing the specific political choices through which structural conditions were translated into political outcomes.

rabbitHole.label: The Cold War
rabbitHole.reason: The Cold War emerged directly from the interwar period and the Second World War it produced — the Soviet-American confrontation was shaped by the ideological battles of the 1930s and the wartime alliance that defeated fascism.

rabbitHole.label: The Weimar Republic
rabbitHole.reason: The Weimar Republic is the central case study within the interwar period — its failure is the event through which the interwar crisis of democracy most directly produced catastrophe.

rabbitHole.label: The Treaty of Versailles
rabbitHole.reason: The Treaty of Versailles created the political and economic conditions of the interwar period — its terms, perceived injustices, and enforcement failures are the starting point for understanding everything that followed.

rabbitHole.label: The Second World War
rabbitHole.reason: The Second World War was the interwar period's ultimate consequence — the failure of collective security, democratic resilience, and economic stability across those twenty years produced the most destructive war in human history.

rabbitHole.label: The Great Depression
rabbitHole.reason: The Great Depression was the central event of the interwar period's second decade — the economic catastrophe that destroyed the political stability of the 1920s and created the conditions for fascism's triumph.

rabbitHole.label: The Soviet Union under Stalin
rabbitHole.reason: The Soviet Union's trajectory during the interwar period — forced industrialization, collectivization, the Terror — was one of the three major political experiments of the period alongside liberal democracy and fascism.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: Nationalism — ethnic, economic, and imperial — was the dominant political force of the interwar period, driving both the democratic failures and the fascist movements that exploited those failures.

reference.author: Steiner, Zara
reference.title: The Lights That Failed: European International History 1919–1933
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of the 1920s — Steiner recovers the genuine possibilities for stable international order in the decade and analyzes why they were not realized. The first volume of the essential two-volume history.

reference.author: Steiner, Zara
reference.title: The Triumph of the Dark: European International History 1933–1939
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of the 1930s — analyzes how Hitler's Germany dismantled the collective security system step by step and why the democracies failed to stop it.

reference.author: Hobsbawm, Eric
reference.title: The Age of Extremes: The Short Twentieth Century 1914–1991
reference.annotation: Hobsbawm's synthesis of 20th-century history treats the interwar period as its central 'age of catastrophe' — the most influential single-volume account, essential despite its structural determinist limitations.

reference.author: Eichengreen, Barry
reference.title: Golden Fetters: The Gold Standard and the Great Depression
reference.annotation: The essential economic history — Eichengreen's argument that the gold standard transmitted deflationary shocks and prevented recovery is the standard account of why the Depression was so severe and so international.

reference.author: Tooze, Adam
reference.title: The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy
reference.annotation: Essential for understanding the economic history of the interwar period through the German economy — Tooze shows why Nazi rearmament was a gamble on conquest and how it shaped Hitler's strategic choices.

commerce.title: The Age of Extremes
commerce.author: Eric Hobsbawm
commerce.note: The essential single-volume account — Hobsbawm's sweep and analytical power make this the best starting point despite its structural limitations.

commerce.title: The Lights That Failed
commerce.author: Zara Steiner
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account of the 1920s — recovers the genuine possibilities for stability and why they failed.

commerce.title: The Sleepwalkers
commerce.author: Christopher Clark
commerce.note: Clark's account of how Europe went to war in 1914 is essential context — the war that produced the interwar period.

commerce.title: Homage to Catalonia
commerce.author: George Orwell
commerce.note: Orwell's account of fighting in the Spanish Civil War — the most vivid witness testimony to the interwar period's ideological intensity.

commerce.title: The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy
commerce.author: Adam Tooze
commerce.note: The essential economic history of Nazi Germany and the interwar period — Tooze's account of how Hitler's economic decisions drove German foreign policy is the standard reference for understanding the relationship between economics and the road to war.

popularCulture.title: Cabaret (1972) and The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014)
popularCulture.description: Both films use the interwar period as a setting that captures the era's combination of decadent vitality and impending catastrophe. Cabaret's Weimar Berlin and The Grand Budapest Hotel's fictional Central European republic both use the formal pleasures of their genres to convey the experience of a world being destroyed from within.

popularCulture.title: The literature of disillusionment
popularCulture.description: The interwar period produced some of the most important literature of modern experience — Hemingway's The Sun Also Rises, Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby, Woolf's Mrs Dalloway, Remarque's All Quiet on the Western Front, Orwell's Down and Out in Paris and London. These works share a sensibility of disillusionment with the Victorian certainties that the First World War had destroyed, and an attempt to find new forms adequate to new experience.

popularCulture.title: Appeasement as political epithet
popularCulture.description: The Munich Agreement of 1938 gave 'appeasement' its modern meaning: dangerous concession to an aggressive power that produces emboldened aggression. Chamberlain's claim to have achieved 'peace for our time' became, within a year, a byword for catastrophic misjudgment.

popularCulture.title: The Great Gatsby as cultural shorthand for the 1920s
popularCulture.description: Fitzgerald's novel is the primary cultural image of interwar American prosperity. What it reveals: the 1920s boom was accompanied by profound anxiety about class and the American dream. What it distorts: Fitzgerald's world is narrowly WASP and wealthy, largely invisible to working class, immigrant, and Black Americans living through the same decade.

popularCulture.title: Peaky Blinders (2013–2022)
popularCulture.description: The BBC series follows a Birmingham criminal gang across the interwar decades. What it captures: the psychological damage of the First World War and the appeal of strongman politics. What it distorts: the Shelby family's power romanticizes the criminal milieu and understates how thoroughly the period's political violence was organized by ideological movements.
